does: DriveSafe presents a framework that enhances risk detection in driving scenarios by generating spatially grounded, multimodal-enriched captions from driving scenes, then using these descriptions to identify hazards, their locations, and unsafe behaviors while providing actionable safety suggestions. The method integrates motion, spatial, and depth cues into natural language descriptions, then fine-tunes a lightweight adapter module using caption-risk pairings to inject domain-specific knowledge into multimodal language models. The framework demonstrates that this structured intermediate representation approach outperforms zero-shot applications of general-purpose MLLMs on fine-grained, spatially grounded risk assessment tasks in autonomous driving contexts.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research applying multimodal large language models to safety-critical applications, particularly in autonomous driving perception. It responds to limitations identified in zero-shot MLLM performance on domain-specific tasks by combining techniques from recent vision-language research with structured scene understanding approaches developed in autonomous driving perception. The paper draws on prior work showing that intermediate representations and explicit spatial grounding improve model performance, as well as adapter-based fine-tuning methods that efficiently inject domain knowledge without full model retraining. It extends prior research on multimodal fusion for autonomous driving by emphasizing the role of natural language as an intermediate reasoning layer between raw perception and safety-critical decision making.
enables: DriveSafe establishes a pathway for integrating natural language reasoning into safety-critical autonomous systems by demonstrating that structured, spatially grounded descriptions can serve as an effective bridge between general-purpose multimodal models and domain-specific requirements. This framework could enable more interpretable risk assessment systems where safety decisions are grounded in explicit, human-understandable descriptions of scene elements and their hazards. The approach suggests future work in generating explanation traces for autonomous vehicle decisions, developing interactive safety systems that provide drivers with contextual hazard awareness, and extending the method to other safety-critical domains. It also points toward more efficient ways of adapting large models to specialized tasks through structured intermediate representations rather than task-specific fine-tuning of entire architectures.
